title: About
text:
This project combines historical and real-time financial data with AI to produce automated research reports for US growth stock investors. By feeding macroeconomic indicators, equity market data, and commodity prices into an AI model, each report identifies historical patterns, draws parallels to current conditions, and highlights risks and opportunities for growth-oriented portfolios.
All charts and visualizations in the reports are created by the author — the AI generates only the written analysis.

title: About
text:
Data Sources
Every report is grounded in real data from:

FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data): Inflation (CPI), GDP growth, unemployment, Fed balance sheet, Treasury yields, household debt, recession indicators, Fed funds rate, and high-yield credit spreads
Yahoo Finance: MSCI World Index, S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, Dow Jones, VIX, gold, and Brent crude oil
Multpl.com: S&P 500 trailing P/E ratios

title: About
text:
Disclaimer
This site is for informational and educational purposes only. Nothing here constitutes financial advice. The AI-generated analysis may contain errors, hallucinations, or outdated information. Always do your own research and consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.

title: Market Research
text:
The Golden Stagflation Trap: Navigating Late-Cycle Growth in 2026
Global equity markets have entered a volatile consolidation phase in the first quarter of 2026, with the Nasdaq-100 posting a -0.6% year-to-date return as of March 5, 2026, while the S&P 500 remained marginally positive at 0.4% nasdaq.com. Volatility has surged, with the VIX climbing 41.5% quarter-to-date to 21.2, reflecting investor anxiety over geopolitical escalation in the Middle East and oil prices topping $99 per barrel tradingeconomics.com. Despite the IMF projecting steady 3.3% global growth for 2026, divergent forces are at play; US inflation is returning to target gradually, but bond yields have stabilized near 4.1%, capping multiple expansion for rate-sensitive assets imf.org.



MSCI World Equity Index


Turning to the US market, the current valuation landscape echoes the late-stage dynamics of the 1999-2000 dot-com era, though with distinct macroeconomic undercurrents. As of March 2026, the S&P 500 trailing P/E ratio stands at 27.97, a level exceeded only by the peaks of the dot-com bubble and the 2021 liquidity boom. This valuation premium is difficult to reconcile with the growth trajectory implied by recent GDP data, suggesting that prices are discounting a soft landing that may not materialize. While earnings have supported these multiples, any disappointment in forward guidance could trigger a sharp compression, particularly for unprofitable growth names that rely on distant cash flows.



S&P 500 PE Ratio


The macroeconomic backdrop presents a more concerning picture than the headline inflation number suggests. While CPI inflation cooled to 2.4% as of February 2026, real GDP growth decelerated sharply to 0.7% in Q4 2025. This stagnation resembles the pre-recession slowdowns of the early 1970s and 2000, where top-line growth faltered before labor markets deteriorated. Unemployment has ticked up to 4.4% as of February 2026, which, combined with sub-1% GDP growth, signals we are likely in the late innings of the economic cycle. Historically, growth stocks underperform during such periods as investors rotate toward quality and cash flow certainty.



US GDP Growth Rate (YoY)


Perhaps the most alarming divergence in our dataset is the behavior of hard assets, which implies significant underlying monetary stress. Gold prices have skyrocketed to 4,755 USD/oz as of April 2026, a level more than double the 2020-2021 peaks, indicating a profound lack of confidence in fiat purchasing power or extreme geopolitical hedging. Simultaneously, Brent crude oil is trading at 102.67 USD/barrel as of April 2026, reintroducing cost-push inflationary pressures just as growth slows. This combination of soaring commodity prices and stalling GDP is the textbook definition of stagflation, a regime that has historically been toxic for high-multiple technology stocks.



Gold Prices


Credit markets, however, have not yet priced in a distress scenario, offering a potential counter-narrative. The BofA High Yield Option-Adjusted Spread sits at 3.46% as of March 2026, which remains relatively tight compared to historical crisis averages above 5%. Additionally, the Federal Reserve has begun easing, with the target rate down to 3.75% as of December 2025 from peak levels, and the yield curve has steepened with the 10-year Treasury at 4.24% versus the 2-year at 3.71% as of March 2026. This normalization suggests that while liquidity is improving, the market is not anticipating an immediate credit event, potentially providing a floor for equity valuations despite the macro headwinds.



Performance of Major US Stock Indices


Based on the patterns identified above, specifically the dot-com era valuation similarity and the 1970s-style commodity shock, we have identified the following risks and opportunities for growth investors:

Risk: Multiple Compression from Stagflation: With the S&P 500 P/E at 27.97 and oil above $100, margin expansion is unlikely. If GDP remains near 0.7% while input costs stay high, earnings estimates will be revised down, leading to a de-rating of growth stocks similar to the 2000-2002 period.
Risk: Liquidity Illusion: While Fed assets are normalizing, the extreme price of gold (4,755 USD/oz) suggests smart money is hedging against currency debasement. If this triggers a bond market revolt, yields could spike despite Fed cuts, crushing long-duration assets.
Opportunity: Quality Growth Rotation: The tight credit spreads (3.46%) indicate that solvent companies can still refinance. Growth stocks with strong free cash flow and low debt will outperform speculative names as the cost of capital remains elevated in real terms.
Opportunity: Rate Sensitive Recovery: With the Fed funds rate at 3.75% and cutting, sectors that were oversold during the 2022-2024 hiking cycle may see a tactical rebound, provided inflation does not re-accelerate from the commodity shock.

Given the elevated valuations, slowing GDP, and stagflationary signals from commodities, we recommend a defensive posture. We suggest a 25-50% portfolio exposure to US growth stocks. This allocation allows for participation in potential liquidity-driven rallies but limits downside risk should the stagflationary pressures intensify. Investors should prioritize companies with pricing power to pass on energy costs and strong balance sheets to weather the credit cycle, avoiding speculative tech that requires perpetual multiple expansion to justify current prices.
